stem: Using the trigonometric substitution \( x = [[a]]\sin\theta \), evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[a]]} [[c]]\sqrt{[[asq]] - x^{2}}\,dx \).
solution: With \( x = [[a]]\sin\theta \), \( \sqrt{[[asq]] - x^{2}} = [[a]]\cos\theta \) and \( dx = [[a]]\cos\theta\,d\theta \), so the integral becomes \( [[c]][[a]]^{2}\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\cos^{2}\theta\,d\theta = [[c]][[a]]^{2}\cdot\tfrac{\pi}{4} = [[answer]] \) (the area of a quarter disk of radius \( [[a]] \), scaled by \( [[c]] \)).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( [[a]] \) instead of \( [[a]]^{2} \), forgetting to square the radius.
This uses \( \tfrac{\pi}{2} \) (a half disk) instead of \( \tfrac{\pi}{4} \) for the quarter disk.
This halves the correct value.
This triples the correct value.
This scales the correct value by 2/3.
This drops the factor of \( \tfrac{1}{4} \) entirely.

stem: The upper limit below is only half of the radius, so the region swept out is a circular segment, not a full quarter disk: apply \( x = [[a]]\sin\theta \) and evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[half]]} [[c]]\sqrt{[[asq]] - x^{2}}\,dx \), keeping BOTH the algebraic and the arcsine pieces of the antiderivative.
solution: With \( x = [[a]]\sin\theta \), the upper limit \( x=[[half]] \) corresponds to \( \sin\theta = \tfrac12 \), i.e. \( \theta=\pi/6 \). The antiderivative \( \tfrac{x}{2}\sqrt{[[asq]]-x^2} + \tfrac{[[asq]]}{2}\arcsin\!\tfrac{x}{[[a]]} \) evaluated from \(0\) to \([[half]]\) gives \( [[asq]]\left(\tfrac{\sqrt3}{8}+\tfrac{\pi}{12}\right) \), so the integral is \( [[c]]\left([[asq]]\left(\tfrac{\sqrt3}{8}+\tfrac{\pi}{12}\right)\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the sector (arcsin) term and drops the triangular \( \sqrt{3}/8 \) piece.
This keeps only the algebraic triangular piece and drops the arcsin sector term.
This mistakenly reuses the full quarter-circle answer \( \pi/4 \) as if the upper limit were \( [[a]] \) instead of \( [[half]] \).
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Using the trigonometric substitution \( x = [[a]]\tan\theta \), evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[a]]} [[c]]\sqrt{[[asq]] + x^{2}}\,dx \).
solution: With \( x = [[a]]\tan\theta \), \( \sqrt{[[asq]]+x^2} = [[a]]\sec\theta \) and \( dx = [[a]]\sec^2\theta\,d\theta \), turning the integral into \( [[c]][[asq]]\int_0^{\pi/4}\sec^3\theta\,d\theta \). Evaluating the standard secant-cubed reduction gives \( [[c]][[asq]]\left(\tfrac{\sqrt2}{2} + \tfrac12\ln(1+\sqrt2)\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the \( \sqrt2/2 \) piece from the secant-cubed antiderivative and drops the logarithmic term.
This keeps only the logarithmic piece (without its \( 1/2 \) coefficient) and drops the algebraic \( \sqrt2/2 \) term.
This mistakenly reuses the \( a^2-x^2 \) circle-area formula for this \( a^2+x^2 \) integrand.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This adds an extra spurious half-term to the correct value.

stem: Using the trigonometric substitution \( x = [[a]]\sec\theta \), evaluate \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[hi]]} [[c]]\sqrt{x^{2} - [[asq]]}\,dx \).
solution: With \( x = [[a]]\sec\theta \), \( \sqrt{x^2-[[asq]]} = [[a]]\tan\theta \) and \( dx = [[a]]\sec\theta\tan\theta\,d\theta \). The antiderivative \( \tfrac{x}{2}\sqrt{x^2-[[asq]]} - \tfrac{[[asq]]}{2}\ln\left|x+\sqrt{x^2-[[asq]]}\right| \), evaluated from \( x=[[a]] \) (where the root vanishes) to \( x=[[hi]] \), gives \( [[answer]] \); the \( \ln [[a]] \) pieces from each endpoint cancel, leaving a value independent of \( \ln [[a]] \) itself.
distractor_rationales: This drops the logarithmic term entirely and keeps only the algebraic \( \tfrac{1}{2}(m^2-1) \) piece.
This uses \( \sqrt{m^2-1} \) without the correct \( \tfrac12 \) scaling and without the log correction.
This halves the correct value.
This doubles the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
